projects.title: Arkaiv — AI-Powered Personal Operating System
projects.subtitle: React, Gemini, Sheets, OpenClaw
projects.author: Akhdan Hanif
projects.toolbox: React 19, Vite, Tailwind CSS 4, Express.js, Google Gemini AI, Google Sheets API, Google Calendar API, OpenClaw AI Agents (Scrum)
projects.techniques: Full-stack development, AI multi-model routing, Local-first data architecture, PKM, AI-orchestrated Scrum, PWA, Framer Motion, Recharts
projects.industries: Personal Productivity, Personal Finance, Self-Quantification, Creator Tools
projects.results: A complete personal OS with 8 integrated views backed by Gemini AI — local-first, PWA-capable, actively maintained at notes-dump-zeta.vercel.app.
projects.content: ## Arkaiv — Ngarsip Harian, Unpacked > "How can I build one system that truly understands my daily life and helps me manage it better?" In 2026, I created **Arkaiv** (short for *Ngarsip Harian* or Daily Archiving). It started as an easy place to track my daily life. Over time, it grew into my complete personal operating system. ![Brain dumping](https://github.com/akhdanzaman/akhdan-projects/blob/main/img/Screenshot%202026-05-26%20043858.png?raw=true) Arkaiv connects 8 different views in one place: Money, Notes, Tasks, Shopping, Calendar, Focus, Library, and Summary. It uses Google Gemini AI and works with a local-first design synced to Google Sheets. **Core Insights** - One single app removes the stress of using many separate tools - AI is most helpful when it quietly watches and gives useful suggestions at the right time - Google Sheets can work very well as a simple, free, and easy-to-read storage - Using the right AI model for each task keeps the app fast and low cost - Managing AI agents with Scrum helped me build strong software quickly, even working alone - Learning my own habits and creating focused work modes improved my daily consistency ### Insight #1: From Simple Notes to Full Life System I used to switch between many apps like Notion, Excel, and habit trackers. It felt messy and tiring. Arkaiv began as a notes tool but slowly became one central place for everything in my daily life. I chose a local-first approach. This means the app works fast in the browser and can be used offline. All data is also saved to Google Sheets as a backup. The data is easy to read and free to store. ![Money tracking](https://github.com/akhdanzaman/akhdan-projects/blob/main/img/Screenshot%202026-05-26%20043402.png?raw=true) ### Insight #2: Making AI Simple and Smart I built a smart system that chooses the best AI model for each job: - Simple tasks use **Gemini Flash** (very fast and cheap) - Complex tasks use **Gemini Pro** (more powerful) The AI also learns my patterns. For example, if I always mark "Kopi Kenangan" as a coffee expense, it remembers and stops asking next time. This saves time and makes the app feel smoother. ### Insight #3: Focus and Daily Life Integration One feature I really like is the Focus view. It turns my tasks into realistic time blocks and automatically adds them to my Calendar. This helps me protect time for deep work. All views work together. The app can show how my spending, tasks, and schedule connect. It gives me clear warnings and suggestions based on my real situation. ### Insight #4: Building with AI Agents using Scrum I acted as the project leader and worked with AI agents inside OpenClaw. We followed Scrum methods with clear plans, regular check-ins, and testing. This helped me create clean, well-documented code much faster than working alone. ### Tech & Design Highlights - **Frontend:** React 19 + Vite 6 + Tailwind CSS with smooth animations - **AI Engine:** Google Gemini with smart model selection - **Data Storage:** Local-first design + Google Sheets sync - **Integrations:** Google login - **Extra:** Works offline as a Progressive Web App (PWA) - **Design:** Clean, calm, and easy to use every day **Live Project:** Arkaiv ### Strategic Takeaways Arkaiv shows that a personal tool built with care and modern AI can be better than many paid apps. It helped me develop skills in full-stack development, AI integration, and creating useful daily systems. This project reflects how I like to work: intentional, connected, and always improving. It is now my main daily tool and is still actively maintained.

projects.title: Olist Ecommerce Satisfaction Visualization
projects.subtitle: Power BI, Visualization
projects.author: Akhdan Hanif
projects.content:
## Olist Case Study, Unpacked

> "What truly separates a satisfied customer from a deeply disappointed one?"

Here's a behind-the-scenes look into how I approached one key question.

**Core Insight**

1. A healthy average can hide operational illness
2. Products and sellers aren't the real culprits
3. Delivery delays trigger customer dissatisfaction
4. The shipping phase is the true bottleneck
5. Delay hotspots are route-specific
6. High cost ≠ fast delivery — financial inefficiencies exposed

This journey took me from high-level metrics, through disproven hypotheses, to uncovering a hidden root cause buried deep within operational data.

### Insight #1: Surface-Level Health Can Hide Internal Dysfunction

![Olist Dashboard](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/68727945c3f561671347630c_1.png)

As always, I started from the top. The General Overview Dashboard displayed the company's vitals — Gross Merchant Value (GMV), Total Orders, and Average Review Score. With a solid 4.09 out of 5, leadership could've easily concluded everything was fine.

But seasoned analysts know better: averages can lie. They mask extremes that cancel each other out. That's where the real investigation began.

### Insight #2: It's Not the Products or the Sellers

![Product Analysis](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/6872794533838b79611b3f0d_2.png)

The most logical first suspects? Bad products or bad sellers. Could certain categories be low quality? Were rogue vendors dragging down the experience?

To find out, I visualized performance by segment. For each product category and top seller, I layered sales volume with average reviews and delay rates. The results were surprising: performance lines were largely flat. Top categories (like furniture_decor) weren't notably worse. Top sellers also showed no red flags.

This was a critical pivot. The obvious culprits were innocent. I had to look elsewhere.

### Insight #3: The Turning Point — Delay Drives Dissatisfaction

![On-Time vs Late](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/68727945750d80a157082fbf_3.png)

Next, I turned to the process itself — the customer journey from "Buy" to "Delivered." Here, I found the smoking gun.

I compared satisfaction scores for On-Time vs. Late orders. The contrast was stark: on-time deliveries earned 4s and 5s. Late ones consistently got 1s and 2s. The correlation was too strong to ignore — and more than a correlation, it was a cause.

Logistics was the #1 driver of customer experience.

### Insight #4: Diagnosing the Delay — Where's the Time Lost?

![Logistics Report](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/6872794581428b13e9127d9a_4.png)

Knowing delays were the issue, I asked the next question: Where exactly is time being lost? To answer this, I created the Logistics Report and broke the order journey into three stages:

- Approval (payment confirmation)
- Processing (seller prep)
- Shipping (delivery)

The data showed one clear spike: Shipping time ballooned from an average of 8 days to over 27 for late orders. Processing times barely changed. The bottleneck was transit — the logistics partners.

### Insight #5: Mapping the Epicenter

![Delay Matrix](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/687279445fe95b0ea228de3e_5.png)

But was this a country-wide problem? Or a regional one? Using a Delay Distribution Matrix, I mapped delay rates from seller_state → customer_state. It became clear: only certain routes were severely affected. Especially shipments from São Paulo (SP) to northeastern states.

This reframed the problem: Not "our deliveries are slow" → but "5 critical routes are severely delayed and need urgent attention."

### Insight #6: High Costs, Slow Deliveries — The Worst Combo

![Cost vs Time](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/687279457d0489bf504f8247_7.png)

Lastly, I looked into shipping cost efficiency. Was Olist using cheap shipping partners, and getting what they paid for? I built a scatter plot of Freight Cost vs. Shipping Time. In theory, more expensive should mean faster. But in reality? The chart was chaotic. Too many data points landed in the "Expensive & Slow" quadrant.

The takeaway? Olist was often overpaying for subpar service — a double hit: poor experience and wasted budget.

## Strategic Takeaways: Turning Data into a Roadmap

This analysis transformed a vague business concern into a concrete set of strategic actions. We learned that Olist doesn't need to change its products or pricing — it needs to fine-tune its operations.

Here's the roadmap:

- Audit and renegotiate with logistics partners, focusing on high-cost, high-delay routes
- Enforce stricter SLAs with sellers to maintain consistency, even if they're not the core issue
- Use data as leverage to demand better service or lower cost

Ultimately, this case proves that in modern e-commerce, delivery isn't just a service — it's part of the product.

projects.title: Essential Oils Customer Satisfaction Analysis
projects.subtitle: Python, Machine Learning
projects.author: Akhdan Hanif
projects.techniques: Sentiment analysis, topic modeling, explainable AI, regression modeling, and Kano classification
projects.industries: Consumer wellness, essential oils, and digital marketplaces
projects.results: Targeted product insights, data-driven quality improvements, and a novel pipeline for turning reviews into roadmaps.
projects.content:
## Case Study: Decoding Customer Satisfaction for Essential Oils

> "Can we extract strategic product insights purely from online reviews?"

The question I explored using 23,000+ reviews of essential oil products from Tokopedia. My goal: identify what truly drives satisfaction in this growing wellness niche and what breaks it.

**Core Insight**

- Aroma and packaging are key drivers of 5-star reviews
- Customers are highly price-sensitive, especially mid-tier buyers
- Diffuser support is a "must" for premium consumers
- Most product features act as performance attributes
- The Kano Model + SHAP approach reveals segment-specific strategies

This project turned raw text into actionable insights — and transformed black-box sentiment into a product development compass.

### Insight #1: What People Really Talk About

![BERTopic](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/6872210461b8e8a2b3c69f32_Screenshot%202025-07-12%20154556.png)

I started by scraping 23,068 Tokopedia reviews using a custom Python pipeline. Using BERTopic, I extracted latent themes and grouped them into 11 Customer Satisfaction Dimensions (CSDs) — like aroma, price, packaging, delivery, and service.

### Insight #2: What Drives Ratings

![Sentiment](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/68721ee0b304a9ed752578c3_Screenshot%202025-07-12%20153724.png)

Then came labeling. Using GPT-4o-mini, I matched reviews to their respective dimensions and classified sentiments. Unsurprisingly, Aroma had the strongest influence on 5-star reviews, while Price, Packaging, and Description Accuracy also emerged as decisive.

### Insight #3: Which Model Knows Best?

![MAE](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/68722109e8c17297c0014e28_MAE%20Model.png)

I trained multiple models (Linear, SVR, RF, GBoost, XGBoost) to predict star ratings. XGBoost stood out — with a MAE of 0.125 and balanced performance across segments.

### Insight #4: Explain, Don't Just Predict

Using SHAP, an explainable AI library, I explained what drove those predictions:

- Aroma had the highest positive SHAP impact on ratings
- Delivery condition and price had strong negative SHAP values when expectations weren't met

### Insight #5: What the Kano Model Reveals

I mapped each CSD using SHAP impact into Kano categories:

- Performance: Aroma, Packaging, Quality — more = better
- Must-be: Price (Mid Segment), Diffuser Support (Premium)
- Excitement: Bonus items, Efficacy (when present, delight; when absent, no complaint)

## Segment Insights: What Matters to Whom

### Low-Price Buyers

![Low SHAP](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/6872226bb6ac4d56581cbd77_Screenshot%202025-07-12%20155216.png)

Consumers treat all product attributes as Performance factors. SMEs can boost satisfaction cost-effectively by improving packaging, offering better tracking, or using volume-based pricing.

### Mid-Price Buyers

![Mid SHAP](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/6872226be740983f6e01f40d_Screenshot%202025-07-12%20155228.png)

Price emerges as a Must-be factor — consumers expect fair pricing and penalize deviations. To win this segment, brands should maintain price-to-value quality consistency while adding subtle experiential value through packaging, sampling, or better service.

### Premium Buyers

![Premium SHAP](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/6872226b8df2ae8cbeaf1928_Screenshot%202025-07-12%20155238.png)

Diffuser support becomes a Must-be expectation. To satisfy this segment, SMEs must meet foundational expectations flawlessly while enhancing exclusivity through personalization, superior packaging, and tech integration.

### Final Reflection

This study shows that customer satisfaction is not just a checklist of product attributes but a dynamic spectrum of expectations shaped by price segments and service experiences. By translating unstructured reviews into a strategic roadmap, we give SMEs the power to act on what customers truly value — accurately, scalably, and responsively.

projects.title: Telco Customer Churn Prediction
projects.subtitle: Python, EDA, Machine Learning
projects.author: Aruci Team (Bilal, Akhdan, Dendy)
projects.techniques: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), feature engineering, classification modeling
projects.results: Lower churn, smarter targeting, and service strategies rooted in behavioral data.
projects.content:
## Customer Churn, Unpacked

> "How can a telecom company turn churn into insight?"

**Core Insight**

- Long-tenured customers are more loyal
- Call center and digital services enhance retention
- High-end device users are a churn risk
- Credit-based payment users churn more
- New customers need nurturing within the first 5 months

What signals tell us someone's about to leave? How can we intervene early?

**The Data Story**

Our dataset, from the Data Science Week Competition and enriched for business context, covered 7,000+ customer records from Jakarta and Bandung, featuring fields like tenure, device class, app usage, payment method, and CLTV.

### Insight #1: Most Churn Happens Early

![Churn by Tenure](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/687227ebb6ac4d56581e585d_Screenshot%202025-07-12%20161413.png)

New customers within their first 5 months show the highest churn risk, suggesting weak onboarding or unmet expectations. Companies must treat the first quarter as a critical loyalty-building phase with targeted welcome offers or guided feature adoption.

### Insight #2: Loyalty Lives in Usage

![Service Usage](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/687227eb93dde2a217c84db9_Screenshot%202025-07-12%20161445.png)

Customers who actively use value-added services (e.g., MyApp, Games, Music) are significantly more likely to stay. Companies should promote cross-service usage through personalized nudges and bundled experiences.

### Insight #3: High-End Device = High Risk

![Device Class](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/687227eb630a6b555231f3d8_Screenshot%202025-07-12%20161504.png)

Despite being premium users, high-end device owners churn more often — possibly due to higher expectations and lower switching friction. Tailored VIP programs or priority support could turn this churn risk into a loyalty opportunity.

### Insight #4: Payment Style Matters

![Payment Method](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/687227ebf48c8590a9a9ee7a_Screenshot%202025-07-12%20161534.png)

Credit and pulsa users churn at higher rates, likely due to payment friction or lack of automation. Transitioning these users to digital wallets or auto-pay systems with incentives could improve satisfaction and reduce dropout.

### Insight #5: Call Center Saves Customers

![Call Center](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/687227eb290c7dad4c1b09a1_Screenshot%202025-07-12%20161552.png)

Call center interaction correlates with lower churn, not just due to problem resolution, but because it creates a human touchpoint in a digital service.

## Our Recommendations

1. Nurture Early Tenure — Provide benefits or loyalty points within the first 5 months
2. Engage High-End Users — Offer exclusive plans, concierge support, or early access to new services
3. Bundling for Value — Cross-promote Video + Game services for synergistic stickiness
4. Optimize Payment Methods — Encourage digital wallets with cashback or ease-of-use benefits
5. Expand Call Center Campaigns — Use outbound calling to educate users and promote retention packages

### From Insights to Action: Why We Built a Prediction Model

![Model Performance](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/687117f971cd518b166221a9/687228302a3f1bf28668a00d_Screenshot%202025-07-12%20161729.png)

We trained multiple models and selected XGBoost, which achieved balanced performance across churned vs retained classes. If I could rework the project, I'd focus on making the model a central decision-support system.

projects.title: Health Commodities Supply Chain by USAID Visualization
projects.subtitle: Tableau, Visualization
projects.author: Akhdan Hanif
projects.techniques: Operational dashboarding, multi-year KPI trend analysis
projects.results: Disrupted assumptions, data-backed narratives, and strategic lessons.
projects.content:
## USAID Supply Chain Analysis, Unpacked

> "What's really happening inside the health commodities supply chain run by USAID — and can we prove it with data?"

This was my first-ever visualization project using a proper BI tool. The dataset came from Kaggle — a simulated USAID health commodities supply chain covering years of deliveries across multiple countries.

**Core Insight**

- A sudden delivery volume collapse in 2015 raised immediate red flags
- Air charter was eating up the budget disproportionately
- RDC (Regional Distribution Center) share was shrinking year over year
- Nigeria dominated the distribution map — but was that strategic or reactive?
- Reliability and cost efficiency aren't just tracked separately — they feed each other

### Insight #1: The 2015 Delivery Collapse — What Happened?

The first thing that jumped out of the data was a massive delivery volume drop in 2015. Not a gradual decline — a cliff. My initial reaction was data quality issues. But after cleaning and verifying, the pattern held. Something structural changed that year.

### Insight #2: Air Charter Was the Budget Killer

Among all shipping methods, air charter stood alone at the top of the cost chart. The per-shipment cost was orders of magnitude higher than ocean or truck freight. Yet the volume going through air wasn't proportional to the spend.

### Insight #3: RDC Distribution Was Shrinking

Regional Distribution Centers are supposed to be the backbone of efficient last-mile delivery. But the data showed their share of total volume declining each year. If RDCs are losing ground, what's replacing them?

### Insight #4: Nigeria Was the Elephant in the Room

Nigeria consistently received the largest share of shipments by a wide margin. Was this allocation proportional to actual need, or was it path-dependent?

### Insight #5: The Gap I Couldn't Close — Reliability and Cost Efficiency

After finishing the first dashboard, I realized I'd only scratched the surface. I was tracking volumes and costs, but I wasn't answering the questions that actually matter in supply chain management:

- How do you measure reliability? Consistency, on-time rate, accuracy
- How do you measure cost efficiency? Shipment count, weight per shipment, freight cost per unit
- What actually drives reliability and cost? Shipping method and vendor selection

This gap analysis became the foundation for the dashboard redesign.

## Strategic Takeaways

This project taught me that a first draft dashboard is never the final answer. The real value comes from stepping back and asking: what question did I not answer? Then going back and answering it.

projects.title: Maintenance Schedule and History Visualization
projects.subtitle: Looker, Visualization
projects.author: Akhdan Hanif
projects.toolbox: Looker Studio, Script App, Google Spreadsheets
projects.techniques: Operational dashboarding, multi-year KPI trend analysis
projects.results: Visualization of Maintenance schedule and report
projects.content:
## First Hands-on Looker Studio

In this project, I was doing a practice, with my experience in Tableau and Power BI to try mocking up a Maintenance dashboard.

The schedule maintenance data used in this dashboard is dummy at the moment but based on real machine failure history in an industry.

### Dashboard Emphasis

Consists of 2 pages, for quick overview and detailed report.

#### Page #1: Maintenance Schedule Quick Overview

This page uses color-coded grids to present scheduled maintenance activities, allowing users to quickly assess the status of each machine's maintenance.

A key feature here is a button connected to Google App Scripts, which enables users to directly manipulate maintenance status in the source spreadsheet. This is especially useful because visualization tools like Looker Studio are typically optimized for historical reporting, not real-time interaction.

#### Page #2: Maintenance Detailed Report

This page provides an in-depth analysis of machine failures and maintenance trends throughout the year. It features visualizations such as a bar chart of downtime per machine, a failure type breakdown, and workload distribution by PIC.

Overall, this project is used for me to learn and prepare if I were asked to do visualization in Looker Studio.

projects.title: Mental Health Prediction App
projects.subtitle: Python, Machine Learning
projects.author: Yukai Mojikai Team (Izzan, Akhdan, Bagus, Rafi, Rifqi)
projects.techniques: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), Data Preprocessing, NLP Modeling
projects.results: Journaling App with Mental Health Analysis
projects.content:
## Mental State Classification Using BERT

Understanding human sentiment is no longer limited to surveys and guesswork. In this project, I led a development of a machine learning pipeline that classifies user text into five mental sentiment categories, using a pre-trained BERT model and TensorFlow.

## Starting with the Problem

Modern digital platforms generate vast amounts of unstructured user feedback. Extracting meaningful emotional signals from this data can guide product decisions, mental health interventions, or conversational AI responses.

## Data Preprocessing

The first step was preparing the data for the BERT model:

- Removing punctuation, numbers, whitespace, special characters, and stop words
- Tokenizing the text using BERT's tokenizer
- Padding all sequences to a uniform length of 53 tokens
- Creating attention masks to distinguish between valid tokens and padding

## Building the Model

The core of the project used a pre-trained BERT model from HuggingFace, combined with custom layers in TensorFlow:

- Input layers for input_ids and attention_mask
- BERT layer producing contextual embeddings
- A global max pooling layer to reduce sequence outputs
- Dense layers with ReLU activations and dropout for regularization
- A final output layer using sigmoid activation for multiclass classification

We got 88.4 score on this project.

## Serving and Deployment

For real-time inference, the model was deployed as a web service on Google Cloud Run. The serving pipeline accepted raw text as input, applied preprocessing, and returned predicted sentiment classes through a REST endpoint.

## Final Thoughts

This project demonstrates a complete machine learning pipeline for NLP tasks. In the end, I built not just a model, but an end-to-end sentiment classification service that can help organizations monitor emotional signals from user text across platforms.
